audit-t01.text: What is BOLA (Broken Object Level Authorization)?
options: An API checks you're logged in but not that you own the object you're requesting
audit-t01.explanation: BOLA lets any authenticated user fetch another user's data by referencing its ID.

audit-t01.text: Where does BOLA rank in the OWASP API Security Top 10?
audit-t01.explanation: Broken Object Level Authorization is API1, the top API risk.

audit-t01.text: What's the primary way to test for BOLA?
options: Swap object IDs between two authenticated test accounts
audit-t01.explanation: If account A can read account B's object by changing the ID, that's BOLA.

audit-t01.text: What made the Peloton API BOLA especially severe?
options: Some endpoints required no authentication at all
audit-t01.explanation: Unauthenticated endpoints returned private profile data to anyone.

audit-t01.text: Is it enough to test only the documented endpoints?
options: No — undocumented endpoints must be enumerated too
audit-t01.explanation: Hidden endpoints are still reachable and must be tested.

audit-t01.text: How should an API respond when user A requests user B's object?
options: HTTP 403 Forbidden with no object data
audit-t01.explanation: Deny with 403 and return nothing — never leak the object.

options: Verify object ownership/authorization on every request, server-side
audit-t01.explanation: A valid token isn't enough — check that this caller may access this object.

audit-t01.text: The lesson of an exposed API is…
options: An API that trusts the caller to name the objects will return every object
audit-t01.explanation: Authentication ≠ authorization; ownership must be enforced.

audit-t02.text: Why is a secret committed to git no longer a secret?
options: It lives in git history on every clone and fork
Only the author can see it
audit-t02.explanation: A committed credential is effectively public, with a timestamp.

audit-t02.text: Does `git rm` on a secret-containing file remove the secret?
options: No — it stays in history on every clone and fork
audit-t02.explanation: You must rotate the secret and rewrite history; rm alone leaves it in the log.

audit-t02.text: How do HashiCorp Vault dynamic secrets reduce risk?
options: They generate short-TTL credentials per request, so a stolen one expires fast
They print secrets to the console
audit-t02.explanation: Short-lived, per-request credentials make a leaked static secret far less useful.

audit-t02.text: What enabled lateral movement in the 2022 Uber breach?
options: A hardcoded admin credential in a PowerShell script on an internal share
audit-t02.explanation: A hardcoded admin secret in an internal script gave the attacker the keys.

audit-t02.text: Where must a secrets-scanning program look?
options: Repos, CI/CD variables, container images, file shares, and internal scripts
audit-t02.explanation: Secrets hide everywhere; scan the whole software supply chain.

audit-t02.text: What's a recommended max TTL for automated systems pulling from Vault?
audit-t02.explanation: Short TTLs (≈1 hour) limit the window a leaked credential is valid.

audit-t02.text: When a secret is found in git, the first step is to…
options: Rotate (revoke and reissue) the secret — assume it's compromised
audit-t02.explanation: Once exposed, it must be rotated; removing the file doesn't undo the exposure.

audit-t02.text: A secret in source control is best thought of as…
options: A public credential with a timestamp
audit-t02.explanation: Treat any committed secret as already public.

audit-t03.text: What does a cloud account without guardrails resemble?
options: A credit card with no spending limit, pointed at the internet
audit-t03.explanation: Without preventive/detective controls, anything can be misconfigured and exposed.

audit-t03.text: Can an in-account IAM admin override an AWS Service Control Policy (SCP)?
options: No — SCPs override IAM and can't be bypassed within the account
audit-t03.explanation: SCPs set an org-level ceiling no in-account admin can exceed.

audit-t03.text: Which AWS control continuously monitors resources against policies?
audit-t03.explanation: Config Rules evaluate resource compliance continuously, not at a point in time.

audit-t03.text: Which two guardrail failures enabled the 106M-record exfiltration?
options: No Config rule caught the WAF misconfig and no CloudWatch alert fired on S3 call volume
A leaked password and a phishing email
A DDoS and a power outage
A stolen laptop and a weak PIN
audit-t03.explanation: Missing detective controls let the misconfig and the mass S3 access go unnoticed.

options: In all regions, not just us-east-1
audit-t03.explanation: All-region CloudTrail ensures no API activity goes unlogged.

audit-t03.text: Why store CloudTrail logs in a separate account?
options: A compromised admin in the audited account could delete in-account logs
audit-t03.explanation: Cross-account log storage protects the audit trail from a local compromise.

audit-t03.text: SCPs and Config Rules together provide…
options: Preventive guardrails plus continuous detection of drift
audit-t03.explanation: SCPs prevent disallowed actions; Config Rules detect noncompliant state.

audit-t03.text: The core lesson of cloud guardrails is…
options: Continuous, automated controls catch misconfigurations humans miss
Guardrails slow teams down for no benefit
audit-t03.explanation: Automated guardrails are how cloud misconfig is actually contained.

audit-t04.text: Why is AdministratorAccess on a developer account a problem?
options: It's an oversized attack surface — compromise grants full control
audit-t04.explanation: Excess privilege turns one compromised account into a full breach.

audit-t04.text: Why is `iam:CreatePolicyVersion` alone a privilege-escalation path?
options: A user can publish a new policy version granting full admin and attach it to themselves
audit-t04.explanation: Editing a policy version lets a user grant themselves admin.

audit-t04.text: Which permission pair lets an attacker run an instance with a privileged role?
audit-t04.explanation: PassRole + RunInstances lets you launch an EC2 with an admin role attached.

audit-t04.text: What IAM principle did the 2022 Okta breach violate?
options: A third-party vendor support account had broader permissions than its function needed
audit-t04.explanation: Over-permissioned vendor access — a least-privilege failure — enabled it.

audit-t04.text: Access keys older than how long are a security finding?
audit-t04.explanation: Long-lived keys (>90 days) on active users should be flagged and rotated.

audit-t04.text: For applications, what does AWS recommend instead of IAM users with long-lived keys?
audit-t04.explanation: Roles issue short-lived credentials with nothing to leak or rotate.

audit-t04.text: Which principle prevents most IAM privilege-escalation paths?
options: Least privilege — grant only what each principal needs
audit-t04.explanation: Least privilege removes the excess permissions escalation relies on.

options: Not a convenience but an attack surface
audit-t04.explanation: Every extra permission is something an attacker can abuse.

audit-t05.text: What does mounting /var/run/docker.sock inside a container grant?
options: Effectively full control of the host
audit-t05.explanation: The Docker socket lets the container control the daemon — and thus the host.

audit-t05.text: A container spec with `runAsUser: 0` and `privileged: true` means…
options: It runs as root with host kernel capabilities — escape equals root on the node
audit-t05.explanation: Privileged root containers make a process escape catastrophic.

audit-t05.text: How did attackers get in for the 2018 Tesla Kubernetes cryptojacking?
options: A Kubernetes dashboard exposed to the internet with no authentication
audit-t05.explanation: An open, unauthenticated K8s dashboard was the entry point.

audit-t05.text: Are Kubernetes Secrets encrypted at rest by default?
options: No — they're base64-encoded; encryption at rest must be configured
audit-t05.explanation: base64 is encoding, not encryption — enable encryption at rest explicitly.

audit-t05.text: When should container image CVE scanning happen?
options: In CI/CD, before images are pushed to the registry
audit-t05.explanation: Pre-registry scanning keeps vulnerable images out of production.

audit-t05.text: A good container hardening default is to…
options: Run as a non-root user without privileged mode
audit-t05.explanation: Non-root, unprivileged containers shrink the escape blast radius.

audit-t05.text: Why is a privileged container with a host socket 'already escaped'?
options: It can control the host directly, so the isolation is effectively gone
audit-t05.explanation: Such a container has host-level control before any exploit.

audit-t05.text: A container security audit should flag…
options: Root/privileged containers, host mounts, unencrypted secrets, unscanned images
audit-t05.explanation: These are the high-impact container misconfigurations.

audit-t06.text: What's the double-edged nature of Infrastructure as Code?
options: One bad module can misconfigure every environment it deploys
audit-t06.explanation: IaC reproduces config — including misconfigurations — across everything.

audit-t06.text: What does IaC security scanning in CI/CD achieve?
options: Catches misconfigurations before infrastructure is deployed (shift left)
audit-t06.explanation: Scanning the code pre-deploy is the cheapest place to catch issues.

audit-t06.text: Which tools do static security analysis on Terraform?
audit-t06.explanation: tfsec and Checkov scan Terraform templates for misconfigurations.

audit-t06.text: How does tfsec classify a security group allowing ingress from 0.0.0.0/0 on port 22?
options: HIGH — SSH open to the entire internet
audit-t06.explanation: Internet-wide SSH is an automatic significant finding.

audit-t06.text: What single control would have caught Toyota's misconfig on day one (2013)?
options: The AWS Config rule 's3-bucket-public-read-prohibited' running continuously
audit-t06.explanation: Continuous Config monitoring would have flagged the public bucket immediately.

audit-t06.text: How should legacy resources that predate IaC be treated?
options: Imported into IaC management and scanned to the same standard
audit-t06.explanation: Legacy resources must meet current standards too — import and scan them.

audit-t06.text: Why is IaC misconfiguration uniquely dangerous?
options: The same flaw is deployed identically across every environment
audit-t06.explanation: Automation multiplies a single mistake across the fleet.

options: Automated scanning in the pipeline plus importing all resources under IaC
audit-t06.explanation: Pipeline scanning + full coverage keeps misconfig from shipping.

audit-t07.text: What does SAST (Static Application Security Testing) do?
options: Analyzes source code without running it to find flaws like SQL injection
Runs the live app and attacks it
audit-t07.explanation: SAST inspects code statically, before the app is built or run.

audit-t07.text: What confirms a SAST-found vulnerability is actually exploitable?
options: DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing) against the running app
audit-t07.explanation: DAST drills the running app to confirm the hole SAST found is real.

audit-t07.text: What does SCA (Software Composition Analysis) detect?
audit-t07.explanation: SCA flags CVEs in third-party libraries you depend on.

audit-t07.text: What would have prevented the Equifax breach?
options: SCA in the pipeline flagging the vulnerable Apache Struts dependency when the patch dropped
audit-t07.explanation: SCA would have caught the unpatched Struts CVE that was exploited.

audit-t07.text: Why can SCA tools detect Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228)?
options: It exists in a known open-source dependency SCA tracks
audit-t07.explanation: Log4j is a tracked dependency, so SCA flags the vulnerable version.

audit-t07.text: Who should authorize a SAST false-positive suppression?
options: A security engineer, with documented justification
audit-t07.explanation: Suppressions need review and a paper trail to avoid hiding real bugs.

audit-t07.text: The relationship between SAST and DAST is…
options: SAST finds the hole before it's drilled; DAST confirms the hole is real
audit-t07.explanation: Static finds candidates; dynamic confirms exploitability.

audit-t07.text: A complete app-security testing program combines…
options: SAST + DAST + SCA in the pipeline
audit-t07.explanation: Code, runtime, and dependencies all need coverage.

audit-t08.text: What's the risk of a flat network?
options: A single compromised endpoint can reach everything
audit-t08.explanation: No internal boundaries means one foothold reaches the whole network.

audit-t08.text: How did the 2013 Target breach succeed?
options: An HVAC vendor's access sat on the same flat network as the point-of-sale systems
audit-t08.explanation: Lack of segmentation let a vendor foothold reach payment systems.

audit-t08.text: An RDS database in a private subnet should allow port 5432 from…
options: Only the application server's security group
audit-t08.explanation: Source-restrict to the app's security group, not broad ranges.

audit-t08.text: Why enable VPC Flow Logs when auditing segmentation?
options: They're the record of actual traffic between subnets
audit-t08.explanation: Flow Logs let you verify segmentation is real, not just intended.

audit-t08.text: Why does PCI DSS require network segmentation?
options: To shrink the cardholder-data scope and limit lateral movement
audit-t08.explanation: Segmentation reduces the regulated scope and contains breaches.

audit-t08.text: A database subnet with a NAT route allowing outbound internet is…
options: A segmentation gap — DB servers shouldn't have an outbound internet route
audit-t08.explanation: Outbound internet from a DB subnet is an exfiltration path and a finding.

options: Containing the blast radius of a single compromise
audit-t08.explanation: Boundaries stop an intruder from reaching everything at once.

options: Keep sensitive systems off the same network as low-trust access (vendors, IoT)
audit-t08.explanation: Isolate sensitive systems from low-trust connections.

audit-t09.text: Why are DDL/DML-only database audit logs insufficient?
options: They miss read-based exfiltration via SELECT queries
audit-t09.explanation: Logging only writes misses an attacker reading (SELECTing) the data out.

audit-t09.text: What single control would have made the decommissioned-hardware data unreadable?
options: Encryption at rest — data without the key is unreadable even on recovered drives
audit-t09.explanation: Encrypted-at-rest data is useless on hardware recovered without the key.

audit-t09.text: A Snowflake ETL service account granted ACCOUNTADMIN (it only needs INSERT) is…
options: A critical finding — any compromise grants full database access
audit-t09.explanation: Superadmin on a service account turns its compromise into total access.

options: Limits which rows a user sees based on their attributes
audit-t09.explanation: RLS partitions data so a compromised account sees only its rows.

audit-t09.text: Does a dev database holding copies of production PII need encryption at rest?
options: Yes — any DB with sensitive data needs it, regardless of environment label
audit-t09.explanation: Sensitive data requires protection wherever it lives.

audit-t09.text: Effective database audit logging must capture…
audit-t09.explanation: Read logging is essential to detect data exfiltration.

audit-t09.text: A service account should be granted…
options: Only the minimum rights its job requires (e.g. INSERT to staging)
audit-t09.explanation: Least privilege caps the damage of a compromised service account.

audit-t09.text: A database with one role that can read every table means…
options: Every user with it is a potential full data breach
audit-t09.explanation: Broad read roles make any account compromise a mass breach.

audit-t10.text: What's the danger of a 'silent' breach?
options: With no logs capturing it, you won't know for months
audit-t10.explanation: Without logging/monitoring, breaches go undetected for long periods.

audit-t10.text: Why did Equifax's attackers go undetected for 78 days?
options: An SSL inspection appliance had an expired cert and stopped inspecting/logging HTTPS
audit-t10.explanation: An unmaintained (expired-cert) monitoring tool was blind to the traffic.

audit-t10.text: How do you identify a SIEM log-coverage gap?
options: Compare the SIEM's log-source list against the full asset inventory
audit-t10.explanation: Assets not sending logs to the SIEM are blind spots.

audit-t10.text: Beyond configuration, what must a log audit confirm?
options: That sources are actively sending logs, not just set up to
audit-t10.explanation: A configured-but-silent source is still a gap.

audit-t10.text: PCI DSS Requirement 10 mandates log retention of…
options: 12 months, with at least 3 months immediately accessible
audit-t10.explanation: 12 months retained, 3 months hot, is the PCI baseline.

audit-t10.text: A SIEM with 4,000+ unacknowledged alerts and 18-hour MTTA indicates…
options: Alert fatigue — ineffective monitoring (critical MTTA should be minutes)
audit-t10.explanation: Huge backlogs and slow response mean alerts aren't actioned.

audit-t10.text: Equifax's logging failure shows monitoring tools…
options: Must be maintained (e.g. cert renewals) or they silently stop working
audit-t10.explanation: An unmaintained tool is a decorative checkbox, not a control.

options: Full log coverage, verified delivery, retention, and fast response to critical alerts
audit-t10.explanation: Coverage + delivery + retention + timely response together.

audit-t11.text: What's the core principle of Zero Trust?
options: Every request is authenticated and authorized regardless of network location
audit-t11.explanation: Zero Trust drops implicit trust — verify every request, every time.

audit-t11.text: Should devices inside the corporate network be trusted by default under Zero Trust?
options: No — location grants no trust; every request must be verified
audit-t11.explanation: Network location is not a trust signal in Zero Trust.

audit-t11.text: What perimeter-security weakness did SolarWinds exploit?
options: Implicit internal trust that let the attacker move laterally once inside
audit-t11.explanation: Once inside, implicit trust enabled broad lateral movement.

audit-t11.text: How does ZTNA (Zero Trust Network Access) differ from a VPN?
options: It grants application-specific access, not broad network-level access
audit-t11.explanation: ZTNA scopes access to specific apps instead of the whole network.

audit-t11.text: Which represents the advanced Identity tier in CISA's Zero Trust Maturity Model?
options: Phishing-resistant FIDO2 hardware keys replacing SMS/TOTP
audit-t11.explanation: FIDO2 hardware keys are phishing-resistant — the advanced identity control.

options: First major enterprise proof that internal network location grants no extra trust
audit-t11.explanation: BeyondCorp pioneered enterprise Zero Trust at scale.

options: Trust can't be based on being inside a network boundary anymore
audit-t11.explanation: With cloud and remote work, the network edge isn't a trust boundary.

audit-t11.text: Zero Trust most directly defends against…
options: Lateral movement after an initial compromise
audit-t11.explanation: Verifying every request blocks the free lateral movement breaches rely on.

audit-t12.text: How does automated compliance differ from manual audits?
options: Automated is continuous; manual audits are point-in-time snapshots
Manual is continuous; automated is a snapshot
audit-t12.explanation: Automation gives continuous truth; manual review is a moment in time.

audit-t12.text: What would have immediately caught Toyota's decade-long public S3 misconfig?
audit-t12.explanation: A continuously-running Config rule flags public buckets on day one.

audit-t12.text: What does AWS Security Hub provide?
options: A unified compliance score aggregating Config, GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and third-party findings
audit-t12.explanation: Security Hub centralizes findings into one compliance view.

audit-t12.text: When a Config rule auto-remediates (e.g. closes a public bucket), it should…
options: Log the action to provide an audit trail
audit-t12.explanation: Auto-remediation must be logged so the change is auditable.

audit-t12.text: Which describes an immature compliance-automation program?
options: Only 3 of 67 Config rules auto-remediate and findings sit ~47 days before fix
audit-t12.explanation: Few auto-remediations + long dwell times = immature.

audit-t12.text: The value of compliance automation is…
options: Continuous verification instead of a periodic snapshot that drifts out of date
audit-t12.explanation: Continuous checks catch drift the moment it happens.

options: Critical and high-severity rules, with prompt remediation
audit-t12.explanation: Automate the high-impact fixes and remediate promptly.

audit-t12.text: A mature compliance program relies on…
options: Continuous automated checks, a unified score, and logged auto-remediation
audit-t12.explanation: Continuous + unified + auto-remediated + audited is the mature state.
